content: The boiling points of 0.01 M aqueous solution of sucrose, NaCl and $\mathrm{CaCl}_2$ would be
explanation:
Sucrose $\longrightarrow$ No dissociation

$$\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{NaCl} \longrightarrow \mathrm{Na}^{+}+\mathrm{Cl}^{-} \\ & \mathrm{CaCl}_2 \longrightarrow \mathrm{Ca}^{2+}+2 \mathrm{Cl}^{-} \end{aligned}$$

Elevation in boiling point is directly proportional to the number of particles and $\mathrm{CaCl}_2$ solution contains more number of particles.

Hence, $\Delta T_b$ will be maximum for $\mathrm{CaCl}_2$ and so will be the boiling point.

content: The osmotic pressure of solution prepared by dissolving 25 mg of $\mathrm{K}_2 \mathrm{SO}_4$ in 2 litre of water at $25^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ is (Assuming that it is completely dissociated)
explanation:
The explanation for calculating the osmotic pressure of a solution formed by dissolving 25 mg of $\mathrm{K}_2 \mathrm{SO}_4$ in 2 liters of water at 25°C is detailed below:

Mass and Volume Information:

Mass of $\mathrm{K}_2 \mathrm{SO}_4$ dissolved = 25 mg = 0.025 g

Volume of the solution = 2 L

Temperature = 25°C = 298 K

Molar Mass Calculation:

The molar mass of $\mathrm{K}_2 \mathrm{SO}_4$ = $2 \times 39 + 32 + 4 \times 16 = 174 \, \mathrm{g/mol}$

Dissociation in Water:

$\mathrm{K}_2 \mathrm{SO}_4$ dissociates completely as:

$ \mathrm{K}_2 \mathrm{SO}_4 \longrightarrow 2 \mathrm{K}^{+} + \mathrm{SO}_4^{2-} $

Number of ions produced per formula unit = 3 (2 $\mathrm{K}^{+}$ and 1 $\mathrm{SO}_4^{2-}$)

Osmotic Pressure Calculation using Van’t Hoff Factor (i):

Van’t Hoff factor $i = 3$

The formula for osmotic pressure is:

$ \pi = iCRT $

Here, $C = \frac{n}{V}$, where $n$ is the moles of solute, and $V$ is the volume in liters.

Calculate moles of $\mathrm{K}_2 \mathrm{SO}_4$:

$ n = \frac{0.025 \, \mathrm{g}}{174\, \mathrm{g/mol}} $

Substitute the known values into the osmotic pressure formula:

$ \pi = 3 \times \frac{0.025}{174} \times \frac{1}{2} \times 0.0821 \times 298 $

Solving for $\pi$, we find:

$ \pi = 5.27 \times 10^{-3} \, \mathrm{atm} $

Thus, the osmotic pressure of the solution is $5.27 \times 10^{-3} \, \mathrm{atm}$.

content: The density of 3 M solution of NaCl is $1.25 \mathrm{~g} / \mathrm{mL}$. What is the molality of solution?
explanation:
To find the molality of the solution, we use the formula:

$ \text{Molality of solution} = \frac{\text{Mass of solute (in moles)}}{\text{Mass of solvent (in kg)}} $

Given that a 3 M NaCl solution has 3 moles of NaCl in 1 L of solution, we can calculate as follows:

Calculate the mass of the solution:

The density of the solution is 1.25 g/mL.

Volume of the solution = 1 L = 1000 mL.

Mass of 1 L of the solution = Volume × Density = $1000 \, \text{mL} \times 1.25 \, \text{g/mL} = 1250 \, \text{g}$.

Calculate the mass of the solute (NaCl):

Molar mass of NaCl = 58.5 g/mol.

Mass of 3 moles of NaCl = $3 \times 58.5 = 175.5 \, \text{g}$.

Determine the mass of the solvent:

Mass of solvent = Total mass of solution - Mass of solute

Mass of solvent = $1250 \, \text{g} - 175.5 \, \text{g} = 1074.5 \, \text{g} = 1.0745 \, \text{kg}$.

Calculate the molality:

$ \text{Molality} = \frac{3 \, \text{mol}}{1.0745 \, \text{kg}} = 2.7 \, \text{mol/kg} $

Therefore, the molality of the solution is 2.7 mol/kg.

content:
Match the Column I with Column II.

Column I
(Aqueous solutions)		 Column II
(Elevation in boiling point)
A. $$\mathrm{0.0001~M~NaCl}$$ I. 0.02
B. $$\mathrm{0.0001~M~Urea}$$ II. 0.003
C. $$\mathrm{0.001~M~MgCl_2}$$ III. 0.0001
D. $$\mathrm{0.01~M~NaCl}$$ IV. 0.0002

Mark the correct option

options.content: A - IV, B - III, C - II, D - I
A - I, B - II, C - III, D - IV
A - IV, B - II, C - I, D - III
A - II, B - IV, C - III, D - I

explanation:
The correct match is A-IV, B-III, C-II, D-I.

$$T \propto i \times$$ molality

Molality is assumed to be equal to molarity, then $$T \propto i \times M$$

Larger the value of $$i$$ and $$M$$, higher will be the boiling point

$$\begin{aligned} & \text { I. } 0.0001 \mathrm{~M} \mathrm{~NaCl}, i=2, M=0.0001 \\ & \therefore \quad T=2 \times 0.0001=0.0002 \end{aligned}$$

$$\text { II. } 0.0001 \mathrm{~M} ~\text {urea, } i=1, \therefore T=1 \times 0.0001=0.0001$$

III. $$0.001 \mathrm{~M} \mathrm{~MgCl}_2, i=3$$

$$\therefore \quad T=0.001 \times 3=0.003$$

IV. $$0.01 \mathrm{~M} \mathrm{~NaCl}, i=2 \therefore T=2 \times 0.01=0.02$$
